sectionName: Chemical & Physical Foundations of Biological Systems

questions.passage: A laboratory separated three analgesic compounds by reverse-phase HPLC using a nonpolar C18 stationary phase and a polar mobile phase. Compound X eluted at 2.1 min, compound Y at 4.8 min, and compound Z at 8.6 min. Independent partition experiments showed that Z was the most hydrophobic and X the least hydrophobic. The chromatogram also showed that increasing the percentage of organic solvent in the mobile phase shortened the retention time of all three compounds.

explanations.D: Correct. In reverse-phase HPLC, a nonpolar stationary phase retains more hydrophobic compounds longer. Z has the longest retention time at 8.6 min and is identified by partition experiments as the most hydrophobic.
explanations.A: X elutes first, consistent with being least hydrophobic.
explanations.B: Y has intermediate retention, not the greatest hydrophobicity.
explanations.C: The different retention times and partition results show they are not equal.

questions.passageImageCaption: Figure 1. Chromatogram showing three compounds separated by reverse-phase HPLC.

questions.stem: Why does compound X elute first?

choices.A: It interacts least strongly with the nonpolar stationary phase.
choices.B: It has the strongest attraction to C18 chains.
choices.C: It is permanently covalently bound to the column.
choices.D: It is the largest molecule by necessity.

explanations.A: Correct. In reverse-phase chromatography, less hydrophobic compounds prefer the polar mobile phase and spend less time interacting with the nonpolar C18 stationary phase. That makes X elute earliest.
explanations.B: Stronger C18 attraction would increase retention time, not shorten it.
explanations.C: A covalently bound compound would not elute normally.
explanations.D: Retention time here is tied to polarity/hydrophobicity, not necessarily size.

questions.stem: What effect does increasing organic solvent have on retention time?

choices.A: It increases retention time for all compounds.
choices.B: It makes the detector stop working by definition.
choices.D: It makes hydrophobic compounds permanently insoluble.

explanations.C: Correct. The passage states that increasing organic solvent shortened retention times. A less polar mobile phase competes more effectively for hydrophobic analytes, reducing their retention on the nonpolar column.
explanations.B: The detector may still operate; solvent composition primarily changes partitioning.
explanations.D: Hydrophobic compounds are often more soluble in a more organic mobile phase.

questions.stem: Which compound would be expected to have the smallest retention factor under the original conditions?

choices.D: The last peak always has the smallest retention factor

explanations.B: Correct. Retention factor increases with time spent in the stationary phase. Since X elutes earliest, it has the smallest retention factor among the three compounds.
explanations.A: Z elutes last and would have the largest retention factor.
explanations.D: The last peak corresponds to greatest retention, not smallest.

questions.stem: Why is reverse-phase HPLC useful for separating similar organic compounds?

choices.A: Small differences in hydrophobic interactions can produce distinct retention times.
choices.B: It converts all molecules into gases.
choices.C: It separates only by nuclear half-life.
choices.D: It eliminates all interactions with the stationary phase.

explanations.A: Correct. Reverse-phase HPLC exploits equilibrium partitioning between a polar mobile phase and nonpolar stationary phase. Even modest differences in hydrophobicity can shift how long molecules are retained, producing separable peaks.
explanations.B: Gas formation is characteristic of gas chromatography, not required here.
explanations.C: Nuclear half-life is unrelated to chromatographic separation.
explanations.D: Stationary-phase interactions are the basis of retention, not something eliminated.

questions.passage: An unknown organic compound was analyzed by IR spectroscopy and mass spectrometry. The IR spectrum showed a strong absorption near 1715 cm-1, no broad absorption from 3200 to 3600 cm-1, and C-H stretches near 2950 cm-1. The mass spectrum gave a molecular ion at m/z 72. The instructor suggested that the compound was likely a simple ketone rather than an alcohol or carboxylic acid.
questions.stem: What functional group is most strongly suggested by the absorption near 1715 cm-1?

explanations.D: Correct. A strong absorption around 1700 cm-1 is characteristic of a C=O stretch. The spectrum's intense 1715 cm-1 band therefore supports a carbonyl-containing compound.
explanations.A: Alcohol O-H stretches are broad and usually appear around 3200-3600 cm-1.
explanations.B: Amine N-H stretches also appear in the higher wavenumber region, not as a strong 1715 cm-1 carbonyl band.
explanations.C: Alkene C=C stretches are typically weaker and near 1640-1680 cm-1, not the best explanation here.

questions.passageImageCaption: Figure 2. Simplified IR spectrum showing a strong carbonyl absorption near 1715 cm-1.

questions.stem: Why is a simple alcohol unlikely?

choices.C: There is no broad O-H absorption from 3200 to 3600 cm-1.
choices.D: The carbonyl peak is too weak to interpret.

explanations.C: Correct. Alcohols typically show a broad O-H stretch in the 3200-3600 cm-1 region. The passage explicitly states that this broad absorption is absent, making a simple alcohol unlikely.
explanations.A: C-H stretches are common in organic compounds and do not rule out alcohols.
explanations.B: An even molecular ion alone does not distinguish alcohol from ketone here.
explanations.D: The carbonyl peak is described as strong, not too weak.

questions.stem: Why is a carboxylic acid less likely than a ketone?

choices.B: A carboxylic acid should show a very broad O-H absorption.
choices.A: Carboxylic acids never contain carbonyl groups.
choices.D: Carboxylic acids have no IR absorptions.

explanations.B: Correct. Carboxylic acids contain carbonyl groups, but they also usually show a very broad O-H band extending roughly 2500-3300 cm-1. The absence of a broad acid O-H band supports ketone over carboxylic acid.
explanations.A: Carboxylic acids do contain carbonyl groups, so the C=O alone is not enough to exclude them.
explanations.C: Ketones do not have O-H stretches unless another functional group is present.
explanations.D: Carboxylic acids have characteristic IR absorptions.

questions.stem: Which formula is consistent with a simple ketone at molecular ion m/z 72?

explanations.D: Correct. C4H8O has nominal mass 4(12) + 8(1) + 16 = 72 and contains one oxygen, consistent with a simple carbonyl compound. It also has one degree of unsaturation, matching a ketone carbonyl.
explanations.B: C5H12 has mass 72 but lacks oxygen and cannot explain the carbonyl IR band.
explanations.C: C2H4O3 has mass 76, not 72, and has more oxygens than suggested.

questions.stem: Which structure type best matches all reported data?

explanations.A: Correct. The strong 1715 cm-1 absorption, absence of broad O-H, and molecular ion consistent with C4H8O point toward a ketone such as butanone rather than an alcohol, acid, or hydrocarbon.
explanations.B: A primary alcohol should show a broad O-H stretch and lacks a carbonyl.
explanations.C: A carboxylic acid should show a very broad acid O-H band in addition to C=O.
explanations.D: A hydrocarbon cannot explain the oxygen-containing carbonyl absorption.
